source.title: Visual experience and subsequent sleep induce sequential plastic changes in putative inhibitory and excitatory cortical neurons
source.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

confidence_rationale: Key mechanistic paper resolving the circuit-level sequence by which waking experience and subsequent sleep together produce ODP in cat visual cortex. Monocular deprivation first depresses FS interneurons responding to the deprived eye, disinhibiting open-eye excitatory principal neurons. During subsequent NREM sleep, principal neurons fire more and become phase-locked to slow waves. OD shifts are only measurable after sleep -- the waking changes alone are insufficient. Directly supports the necessary-but-not-sufficient framing and provides the two-stage gate mechanism.
failure_signatures: The mechanism linking slow-wave phase-locking to lasting synaptic weight changes is not directly demonstrated at the molecular level.
The finding that OD shifts require sleep does not rule out the possibility that extended wakefulness would also produce equivalent shifts.
The FS interneuron -> principal neuron disinhibition mechanism is cat visual cortex specific and may not generalise to all forms of experience-dependent plasticity.

mapping.source_claim_statement: In freely behaving cats during the critical period, monocular deprivation depresses FS interneurons biased toward the deprived eye, disinhibiting open-eye principal neurons. During subsequent NREM sleep, principal neurons increase firing and become phase-locked to slow waves. OD shifts are only evident after sleep, proportional to MD-induced FS changes and to sleep-associated principal neuron changes, greatest in principal neurons firing 40-300 ms after neighboring FS neurons during slow waves.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-120 and MECH-121 claim the offline phase executes a consolidation program that cannot be replicated during waking. The Aton PNAS paper provides the mechanistic circuit account: waking experience creates an instructive change in the inhibitory network (FS interneuron depression disinhibits open-eye principal neurons), but the actual synaptic strengthening requires slow-wave-phase-locked principal neuron firing that only occurs during NREM sleep. This is a two-stage gate: (gate 1) waking experience determines what is tagged for consolidation; (gate 2) sleep determines when and how the consolidation is written. If waking dynamics produce excessive tagging of dominant trajectories (WTA forward model dominance), gate 1 is corrupted before gate 2 even opens -- sleep cannot consolidate clean instructions when the instructions themselves are biased toward a dominant strategy.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The specific mechanism (FS interneuron depression -> principal neuron disinhibition -> slow-wave phase-locking) is a visual cortex critical period finding. REE's consolidation mechanism is at a different level of abstraction. The implication that 'corrupted waking tagging corrupts sleep consolidation' follows logically from the two-stage gate model but is not directly tested in this paper.
mapping.source_context: Adult cats (critical period postnatal days 28-41); freely behaving; simultaneous recording of FS interneurons and putative principal neurons in extragranular visual cortex across MD and post-MD sleep.

confidence_components.notes: Excellent source quality (PNAS, in vivo simultaneous recordings, Frank lab). Mapping fidelity good for the two-stage gate functional logic; moderate deduction to reach REE architecture. Transfer risk moderate.
